narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, you've put significant effort into pursuing law school, including scoring a 33 on your LSAT and maintaining a 3.00 undergraduate GPA, while coming from a lower-middle income family and planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated your probability of failing at 61.7%—a threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success to maintain their outcomes. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance in school, particularly your grades in later years. Your position in the lowest decile for Year 3 grades stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your Year 1 grades, also in the lowest decile, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting early academic challenges that didn't improve over time. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA played a helpful role, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since your 3.00 is reasonably close to the average of 3.21, showing some resilience in college-level work. Compared to typical students—who often rank around the 5th or 6th decile in both early and later school years—your profile appeared riskier in those key academic metrics, even with your LSAT score being just a bit under the usual 36.7 average. While factors like your full-time status (common for 93% of applicants) and family background didn't sway things much, the model weighed your weaker school deciles heavily against bar passage trends in the dataset. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven flag on patterns linking low decile rankings to higher failure rates. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school grades into higher deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or summer sessions, and aim to retake the LSAT for a score closer to or above average. Building a stronger academic narrative could shift future predictions in your favor, and many applicants in similar situations succeed by addressing these exact gaps before reapplying. You're clearly motivated—use this feedback as a roadmap to strengthen your case next time.
